cot_prefix: Okay, so there's this problem about a teacher and four students. The teacher picks four consecutive two-digit numbers, gives each student a different number from that set. The teacher tells them that there's a number divisible by 6 and another divisible by 7 in the set. Then she asks if any of them can figure out what the set S is. All four students say no. But after hearing that all four said no, they can figure it out. The question is asking for the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S. First, let me parse the problem again. The set S has four consecutive two-digit numbers. So numbers like 10-13, 11-14, ..., up to maybe 97-100? But since they are two-digit numbers, the maximum would be 97, 98, 99, 100? Wait, but 100 is a three-digit number. So the four consecutive numbers must be from 10 up to such that the fourth number is still two-digit. So the highest set is 97, 98, 99, 100? But 100 is three-digit. Therefore, actually the four consecutive numbers must be up to 96-99? Because 96, 97, 98, 99. Then the next one would be 97, 98, 99, 100, but 100 is three-digit. Therefore, the maximum possible set is 96-99. So the greatest element of S can be up to 99. So S is a set of four consecutive two-digit numbers. Each student gets one number. The teacher told them that in S, there is some number divisible by 6 and a different number divisible by 7. So there are two different numbers in S: one divisible by 6, another divisible by 7. Then the teacher asks if any of the students can figure out S. All four students say no. Then, upon hearing that all four said no, they can figure out S.
